findings.summary: classifier/constants.py re-exports REFUSAL_PHRASES as ERROR_PHRASES, adding an unnecessary alias layer
evidence: ERROR_PHRASES: tuple[str, ...] = REFUSAL_PHRASES on line 48 of classifier/constants.py, then quality_gates.py imports ERROR_PHRASES instead of using REFUSAL_PHRASES directly
findings.suggestion: Import REFUSAL_PHRASES directly in quality_gates.py and remove the ERROR_PHRASES alias from classifier/constants.py

findings.summary: init_openai_client() is a trivial one-line passthrough to _openai_holder.init() with no added policy
evidence: Line 44-45 of llm.py: def init_openai_client(client): _openai_holder.init(client) — no validation, logging, or translation; preflight.py already imports _openai_holder directly
findings.suggestion: Either import _openai_holder directly in library_setup.py (consistent with preflight.py) or remove the function; alternatively justify the facade by adding validation

findings.summary: OCR worker class is named DocumentProcessor while classifier worker class is ClassificationProcessor — sibling modules use different naming patterns for the same role
evidence: ocr/worker.py defines DocumentProcessor; classifier/worker.py defines ClassificationProcessor; both serve identical roles as per-document orchestrators instantiated by daemon thread pools
findings.suggestion: Rename OCR's DocumentProcessor to OcrProcessor for consistent domain-prefixed naming across sibling modules

findings.summary: Both workers call reset_stats() on providers that are created fresh per-document, making the reset a no-op
evidence: ocr/daemon.py line 21 creates OcrProvider per-document; worker.py line 80 calls reset_stats() on that fresh instance. classifier/daemon.py line 68 creates ClassificationProvider per-document; worker.py line 84 calls reset_stats(). Both resets zero-out already-zero counters.
findings.suggestion: Remove the reset_stats() calls from both worker process() methods since providers are instantiated fresh per-document, or make providers shared across a batch and reset at batch boundaries

findings.summary: daemon_loop.py catches a specific exception list but misses KeyError from malformed API JSON responses
evidence: Line 106: except (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError) — fetch_work() materializes list(iter_documents_by_pipeline_tag(...)) which accesses response.json() results; a KeyError from unexpected JSON shape would crash the daemon loop entirely
findings.suggestion: Add KeyError to the exception tuple, or use a broader catch (Exception) with re-raise for SystemExit/KeyboardInterrupt to ensure the daemon loop never crashes from transient data issues

findings.summary: Catch clauses for Paperless API calls inconsistently include ValueError — some do, some do not, for equivalent operations
evidence: tags.py get_latest_tags line 41 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError); claims.py line 31, 54, 65 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) without ValueError; stale_lock.py line 27, 54 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) without ValueError — all call the same PaperlessClient methods that can raise the same exceptions
findings.suggestion: Define a shared PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS tuple and use it consistently across all modules wrapping PaperlessClient calls

findings.summary: TaxonomyCache catches bare Exception for API create calls instead of specific exception types used elsewhere
evidence: Line 192: except Exception in _get_or_create_item_id; line 235: except Exception in get_or_create_tag_ids — both wrap PaperlessClient create calls where the expected failures are httpx.HTTPError/HTTPStatusError, but bare Exception masks programming errors
findings.suggestion: Narrow to (httpx.HTTPError, httpx.HTTPStatusError, OSError) to match the error handling convention used in other modules for Paperless API calls
